coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence quenching of photoluminescent organic polymer nanofilms by ferric ions
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Baoxi
Xu, Zhen
Qi, Chenggang
Guo, Xiumei
Gai, Ligang
coredata.dc:description: There are considerable reports on fluorescent detection of ferric ions, which were universally simplified as Fe3+, by using carbon dots-based sensors. Although several mechanisms have been proposed for the fluorescence quenching of the host, it deserves deep understanding on this topic because the hydrolysis of Fe3+ has not been considered in previous reports. In this contribution, we report on fluorescent detection of ferric ions in mixed metal-ion solution and in real water with organic polymer nanofilms (PNFs)-based sensor. The limit of detection of PNFs towards ferric ions is 44 nM. In particular, we focus on demonstration of the fluorescence quenching mechanisms through instrumental analysis and theoretical computations. The electron transfer from the LUMO level of PNFs to the LUMO level of Fe2(OH)2 4+ and the HOMO levels of Fe(OH)2 + is suggested to be responsible for the impressive fluorescence quenching of PNFs by ferric ions with concentration lower than 80 µM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organic polymer nanofilms
Ferric-ion sensing
Quenching mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL quenching of PNFs by metal ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( ZR2019MEM015 and ZR2017QB003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: LSCM images of PNFs in water with λ ex at 405 nm: (a) bright field; (b) dark field; (c) integration of a and b.

(a) optical spectra of PNFs; (b) histogram of D FQ for PNFs without and with metal ions; (c) normalized PL spectra for PNFs without and with metal ions; (d) PL spectra of PNFs as a function of FN concentration; inset in d is the plot of F 0/F vs FN concentration; λ ex for b‒d was set at 360 nm; polymer concentrations for a‒d were set at 0.005 mg mL‒1, at which the absorbance of PNFs at 345 nm was below 0.1; the concentration of every metal ion in b and c was set at 50 μM.
Fluorescence decay curves of PNF aqueous solution and PNF aqueous solution containing 5 and 50 µM ferric ion, respectively; the concentration of PNFs in water is 0.125 mg mL‒1.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of PNFs; (b) XPS survey spectrum; (c) C 1 s; (d) N 1 s; (e) O 1 s; (f) S 2p.

(a,b) Absorption spectra of PNFs without and with ferric ion; the concentrations of PNFs are 0.005 and 5 mg mL‒1 in a and b, respectively; (c) plots of electronegativity and D FQ for the concerned metal ions; (d) absorption spectra of FN aqueous solutions and PL of PNFs (0.005 mg mL‒1); (e) absorption spectra of FN and PNFs‒FN with PNF concentration of 0.005 mg mL‒1; (f) HOMO and LUMO levels of PNFs and ferric ion species abbreviated as Fe2(OH)2 4+, Fe(OH)2 +, FeOH2+, and Fe3+.
Emission spectra of PNF (0.125 mg mL‒1) aqueous solutions without and with FN (5 μM); inset shows the amplified plots.
Comparison of LOD for carbon-based sensors towards ferric ion.
Results of fluorescent detection of ferric ion in water with PNFs.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence quenching of photoluminescent organic polymer nanofilms by ferric ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering & Technology Center of Electrochemistry, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering & Technology Center of Electrochemistry, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aInstitute of Advanced Energy Materials and Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory and Facilities Management Office, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bLaboratory and Facilities Management Office, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There are considerable reports on fluorescent detection of ferric ions, which were universally simplified as Fe3+, by using carbon dots-based sensors. Although several mechanisms have been proposed for the fluorescence quenching of the host, it deserves deep understanding on this topic because the hydrolysis of Fe3+ has not been considered in previous reports. In this contribution, we report on fluorescent detection of ferric ions in mixed metal-ion solution and in real water with organic polymer nanofilms (PNFs)-based sensor. The limit of detection of PNFs towards ferric ions is 44 nM. In particular, we focus on demonstration of the fluorescence quenching mechanisms through instrumental analysis and theoretical computations. The electron transfer from the LUMO level of PNFs to the LUMO level of Fe2(OH)2 4+ and the HOMO levels of Fe(OH)2 + is suggested to be responsible for the impressive fluorescence quenching of PNFs by ferric ions with concentration lower than 80 µM.

ce:sections.ce:para: SAn excited electron from the LUMO level of polymer nanofilms (PNFs) can transfer to the LUMO level of Fe2(OH)2 4+ and the HOMO levels of Fe(OH)2 +, resulting in fluorescence quenching of the host.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferric ions play crucial roles in biological processes such as oxygen transport, enzyme catalysis, and DNA and RNA synthesis [1,2]. However, the ferric ion concentration in organisms influences their healthy conditions. Overdose of ferric ions can cause diseases such as kidney failure, liver damage, diabetes, and anemia etc. [1,2]. Therefore, it is necessary for early diagnosis and identification of the above diseases through evaluation of ferric-ion levels in organisms and environment.

To date, many reports have been documented on detection of ferric ions, which were universally simplified as Fe3+, by using carbon dots (CDs) as sensors [1–11]. Four types of quenching mechanisms concerning CDs with ferric ion have been suggested: (i) coordination interactions between ferric ion and phenolic hydroxyl groups [5,6]; (ii) redox reaction between hydroquinone and ferric ion [7]; (iii) partial transfer of an electron in the excited state of CDs to the d orbital of ferric ion [8,9]; and (iv) coordination together with electron transfer [10,11]. For fluorescence quenching of CDs by other metal ions such as Hg2+, a synergistic quenching mechanism was suggested that includes electrostatic interaction, metal‒ligand coordination, and an electron or energy transfer process [12]. However, the aforementioned fluorescence quenching mechanisms are lack of solid evidences. Thus, it deserves deep understanding on the sensing mechanism concerning carbon-based sensors with ferric ions.

Using citric acid (CA) and L-cysteine (LCy) as the raw materials, CDs with relatively high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) were frequently reported through hydrothermal [13–18], microwave-assisted hydrothermal [19,20], and grinding plus heating approaches [21–23]. Recently, we have reported on synthesis of photoluminescent organic polymer nanofilms (PNFs) in water through a self-assembly formation mechanism based on real-time visualizations in combination with molecular dynamics simulations [24]. The conditions for formation of PNFs in water were optimized that include (1) obtaining polymer powders (PPs) by heating the mixture of CA and LCy with CA/LCy molar ratio of 2:1 at 160 °C for 1 h; (2) dissolving PPs in water with polymer concentration ranging from 1 to 2 mg mL‒1, with the assistance of ultrasound.

In this contribution, we focus on the fluorescence quenching mechanisms concerning PNFs with ferric ions. Five fluorescence quenching mechanisms have been explored through instrumental analysis in combination with theoretical computations.

In a typical synthetic procedure, PPs were prepared by heating the mixture of CA and LCy with CA/LCy molar ratio of 2:1 at 160 °C for 1 h, and then allowed to cool to room temperature. PNF aqueous solution was prepared by dissolving PPs into distilled water with the assistance of ultrasound (100 W) at room temperature for 5 min. PNF powders were obtained after freeze drying of the PNF aqueous solution. As demonstrated in our previous report [24], the nature of PNFs is formed by self-assembled pyridine derivatives which result from intermolecular condensation of CA with LCy.

Laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) images were taken on a Leica SP8 laser scanning confocal microscope. For LSCM observations of PNFs, a drop of PP aqueous solution with concentration of 0.005 mg mL‒1 was placed onto a confocal observation dish. ζ-Potential measurements were carried out on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 apparatus. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu IRPrestige-21 infrared spectrometer using pressed KBr discs. Measurements by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out on a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, using monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV). The high-resolution spectra were calibrated by setting C 1 s at 284.8 eV. Gaussian and Lorentz functions were employed for fitting the high-resolution XPS spectra.

Room-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected on a Hitachi F-4600FL fluorescence spectrometer. The time-resolved fluorescence lifetime was measured through an Edinburgh FLS920 time-correlated single-photon counting spectrometer. Ultraviolet/visible (UV/Vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a TU-1901 UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing, China). Using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference, the values of relative QY were calculated according to Eq. (1) [25,26]:
(1)
Q Y = Q
Y r
×
I
I r
×
A r
A
×
n 2
n
r
2
where r refers to the reference, I, A, and n represent the emission intensity, absorbance intensity, and refractive index of water (n = 1.33), respectively.

The energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of PNFs were estimated through cyclic voltammograms (CVs). The CVs were collected on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instruments Co., China) in a three-electrode system, using Pt sheet as the counter electrode, PNFs-loaded glassy carbon as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and acetonitrile containing 0.1 mol L‒1 (Et)4NBF4 as the electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL quenching of PNFs by metal ions
ce:section.ce:para:
Before PL quenching tests, aqueous solutions of Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Al3+, and Fe3+ with designated ion concentrations were prepared by dissolution of corresponding metal nitrates in distilled water through the volumetric method. The reason for selecting the aforementioned altervalent metal ions is based on (i) the selected metal ions covering groups of IA‒IVA, IB, IIB, and VIII are familiar to readers who are engaged in metal-ion detection through the fluorescence technique; and (2) altervalent metal ions have different electronegativities, which may influence the sensing ability of sensors. In the testing process, an aliquot of PNF aqueous solution was mixed with an aliquot of metal-ion solution, followed by instantly measuring the optical properties of the mixed solution. The degree of fluorescence quenching (D FQ) was calculated according to Eq. (2) expressed as [27]:
(2)
D FQ
=
F 0
− F
F 0
× 100 %
where F 0 and F represent the maximum fluorescence intensity without and with quencher, respectively.

The PL intensities for the inner filter effect (IFE) were corrected according to Eq. (3) expressed as [28]:
(3)
I corr
=
I obs
10
−
ε
Q
c Q
l ′
where I obs and I corr are fluorescence intensities observed and corrected for the absorption of exciting light by the quencher, respectively; ε Q c Q l’ is the absorbance of quencher at the monitoring wavelength with the effective path length l’.

For real applications, quantitative amounts of PPs and ferric nitrate (FN) were added into real water, which includes tap water (Jinan, China), lake water (Changqing Lake, Jinan, China), and sea water (Yellow Sea, Qingdao, China). Before usage, the latter two kinds of water were filtered to remove precipitates. The concentration of PNFs in water is 0.125 mg mL‒1, at which PNFs exhibits the highest PL intensity under excitation at 360 nm. The FN concentration in water is 5 μM.

The configurations and the HOMO and LUMO levels of the hydrolyzates of Fe3+ were determined, using Gaussian 09 program package through a B3LYP method based on the density functional theory at the B3LYP/def2-SVP level. Solvation effects were incorporated by the SMD salvation model with water as solvent [29]. Dispersion corrections were performed by Grimme's D3 version with BJ damping function [30].

The PNFs in-situ formed in water can be confirmed by the LSCM images (Fig. 1 ), which exhibit strong blue luminescence. Fig. 2 shows the optical properties of PNFs in aqueous solution with concentration at 0.005 mg mL‒1. The peaks at 204, 241, and 345 nm in the UV‒Vis absorption spectrum correspond separately to n→σ*, π→π*, and n→π* electron transitions (Fig. 2a) [22,23]. The latter peak at 345 nm is related to the trapping of excited-state energy by surface states and contributes more to fluorescence emissions [14,21], compared with the former two peaks. The excitation spectrum exhibits two peaks centered at 250 and 360 nm (Fig. 2a). The maximum fluorescence emissions are located at 434 ± 3 nm at excitation wavelengths (λ ex) ranging from 240 to 400 nm (Fig. 2a). This result is indicative of a λ ex-independent emission behavior, which may be associated with the pyridine-based organic fluorophores in PNFs [15,23]. The QY of PNFs relative to quinine sulfate standard is 76%, much higher than that (˂ 50%) of most carbon-based luminophors [16,19,31–34].

To evaluate the sensing ability of PNFs towards metal ions, altervalent metal ions were selected which include Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Al3+, and ferric ions. It is found that the ferric ion has the highest ability for fluorescence quenching of PNFs compared with the other metal ions (Fig. 2b). This phenomenon is in accordance with the results for fluorescence quenching of CDs with ferric ion [1–11]. Compared with the PL for individual PNF solution, the PL intensity for PNFs with ferric ion is decreased by 23%, higher than the decrease of 14.5% for PNFs with the total other metal ions (Fig. 2c). When ferric ion coexists with the other metal ions in solution, the decrease in PL intensity of PNFs can reach 40.3%. This result indicates PNFs have the selectivity to sense ferric ion in mixed metal-ion solution.

The PL intensity of PNFs decreases as the ferric ion concentration increases (Fig. 2d). The red shift of the maximum emissions in Fig. 2c and d reflects the interactions between PNFs and metal ions. Using the data derived from Fig. 2d, the quenching rate constant (K q) can be estimated according to the classical Stern‒Volmer equation (Eq. (4)) [17,35]:
(4)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ C ]
where F 0 and F are the steady-state PL intensity without and with quencher, respectively, [C] is the concentration of quencher, and τ 0 is the fluorescent lifetime.

The τ 0 of PNFs in aqueous solution (0.125 mg mL‒1) is 10.75 ns, according to the monoexponential fluorescence decay curve (Fig. 3 ). With addition of 5 and 50 µM FN, the τ 0 of PNFs is changed to be 10.65 and 10.63 ns, respectively. The decrease in τ 0 with addition trace amounts of ferric ions suggests conjugation of PNFs with Fe3+ hydrolyzates is instantly completed and electron transfer may occur between the two components (discussion below).

By linear fit of F 0/F vs [C] (Fig. 2d, inset), the K q for PNFs is calculated to be 2.64 × 1011 L mol‒1 s‒1, higher by one order of magnitude than the maximum scatter collision quenching constant (2 × 1010 L mol‒1 s‒1) [36]. According to the three times standard deviation rule (3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation, and k is the slope of the linear plot) [37,38], the limit of detection (LOD) for PNFs towards ferric ion is estimated to be 44 nM. This LOD value is lower than or comparable with that for CDs-based sensors [2,6–11,25] (Table 1 ), and is much lower than the maximum Fe3+tolerance (5.4 μM) in drinking water permitted by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [6].

Considering the fluorescence quenching for carbon-based sensors with metal ions involves surface phenolic hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups [5–7,10–12], the surface chemistry of PNFs was examined by the FT-IR and XPS techniques. The peaks marked in Fig. 4 a correspond separately to v(O‒H) (3421 cm‒1), v(N‒H) (3200 cm‒1), v(C = O) (1715 cm‒1), v(C = C) (1635 cm‒1), v(C = N) (1520 cm‒1) stretching vibrations, γ(O‒H) (1396 cm‒1) and ρ(O‒H) (878 cm‒1) deforming vibrations, and v(C‒N/O,S) (1221‒1070 cm‒1) stretching vibrations [20–22]. The FT-IR spectrum reveals wealthy functional groups on the surface of PNFs.

The survey XPS spectrum of PNFs evidences the existence of elemental C, N, O, and S (Fig. 4b). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum can be resolved into four components centered at 284.6 (29.4% in total areas), 285.4 (22.1%), 286.3 (19.0%), and 288.3 eV (29.5%) (Fig. 4c), corresponding separately to C = C, C‒C/N,O,S, C = O/N, and HO‒C = O [16,19,21]. Three components centered at 399.7 (30.2%), 400.3 (41.5%), and 401.1 eV (28.3%) can be resolved in the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4d), corresponding separately to C = N/C‒N, N‒H, and N‒C = O [14,16]. The O 1 s spectrum confirms the oxygen-containing surface groups of C‒OH (531.2 eV, 41.4%), C = O (532 eV, 33.2%), and COOH (532.9 eV, 25.4%) (Fig. 4e) [14]. The spin doublet centered at 163.2 (62.3%) and 164.5 eV (37.7%) in S 2p spectrum corresponds to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of C‒S‒H/C (Fig. 4f) [15,21]. Note that there is no peak at ca. 168 eV signaling oxysulfides, which were assigned in S 2p spectrum of CDs derived from hydrothermal reaction of CA with LCy [16]. In brief, the XPS spectra confirm the existence of O-, N-, and S-containing groups anchoring on PNFs, in concert with the FT-IR result.

The color reaction between ferric ion and phenolic hydroxyl was frequently used to interpret the fluorescence quenching of CDs or graphene oxide with ferric ion [38–40]. The color of ferric ion‒phenol complex results from the formation of a type of coordinated negative ions in terms of Fe(OR)6 3‒ in aqueous solution. The formation of Fe(OR)6 3‒ is reflected by a broad absorption peak centered at 500‒600 nm [41]. Also, the shape and position of the peak are not relevant to the counteranions of ferric salts [41]. To verify whether the color reaction is responsible for the fluorescence quenching of PNFs with ferric ion, we comparatively measured the absorption spectra of PNF aqueous solutions containing different concentrations of ferric ion. It is found that the peak centered at 345 nm shifts to lower wavelengths as the concentration of ferric ion increases (Fig. 5 a). A shoulder peak centered at ca. 294 nm occurs as the ferric ion concentration increases to and more than 80 μM. The shoulder peak at 294 nm is characteristic of the hydrolyzates (FeOH2+, Fe(OH)2 +, and Fe2(OH)2 4+) of Fe3+ [42,43]. The formation of the hydrolyzates is based on eqs 5‒7:
(5)
F
e
3 +
+
H 2
O ≪ FeO
H
2 +
+
H
+
(6)
F
e
3 +
+ 2
H 2
O ≪ Fe
( OH )
2
+
+ 2
H
+
(7)
2 F
e
3 +
+ 2
H 2
O ≪ F
e 2
( OH )
2
4 +
+ 2
H
+
Note that there are no absorption peaks in the range of 500‒600 nm corresponding to Fe(OR)6 3‒ complex ion [41], even for PNFs‒FN systems with elevated PNF (5 mg mL‒1) and FN (0.125‒2.5 mM) concentrations (Fig. 5b). The nondetection of the peak at 500‒600 nm suggests the color reaction between ferric ions and the phenolic hydroxyls on PNFs is not responsible for the impressive fluorescence quenching in the present PNFs‒FN systems with FN concentration much lower than 2.5 mM.

Electrostatic attraction was also suggested as a factor that influences the fluorescence quenching of CDs with metal ions [12]. In view of the negatively charged PNFs in water (‒1.7 mV, Fig. S1), addition of metal ions into PNF aqueous solution may cause electrostatic attractions, and hence change the PL of PNFs. The electronegativities of the concerned metal ions without hydrolysis and the corresponding D FQ of PNFs are plotted as shown in Fig. 5c, where the electronegativity values are excerpted from reference [44]. It is found that there is no linear relationship between D FQ and electronegativity. In other words, the fluorescence quenching of PNFs by ferric ions cannot be attributed to the electrostatic attraction.

The IFE is frequently used to interpret the mechanisms in fluorescent assays [45,46]. The IFE is known to result from absorption of the excitation and/or emission of light by absorbers in a detection system [45]. In the present case, the absorption spectra of FN‒PNFs (Fig. 5a) and FN (Fig. 5d), with FN concentration ranging from 5 to 80 µM, exhibit marginal overlaps (< 0.1) with the emission of PNFs (0.005 mg mL‒1) in aqueous solution at the λ ex of 360 nm. This result suggests the IFE is also not responsible for the fluorescence quenching of PNFs by ferric ions with FN concentration lower than 80 µM. On the other hand, the absorption spectrum of PNFs exhibits a negligible overlap with their emission spectrum excited at 360 nm (Fig. 5d). This result excludes the IFE-based self-quenching of PNFs at 0.005 mg mL‒1.

The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism was also proposed for the fluorescence quenching of the host by the quencher [47,48]. The overlap between the absorption spectrum of the quencher and the PL emission spectrum of the host is considered as a precondition for the occurrence of FRET. As aforementioned, the absorption spectra of FN‒PNFs and FN aqueous solutions exhibit negligible overlaps with the emission spectrum of PNFs (Fig. 5d). This result indicates the fluorescence quenching of PNFs by ferric ions does not result from FRET for the PNFs‒FN systems with FN concentration lower than 80 µM.

The hydrolysis of Fe3+ in FN aqueous solution at room temperature is spontaneous, yielding hydrolyzates of FeOH2+, Fe(OH)2 +, and Fe2(OH)2 4+ [42,43]. As the FN concentration increases, the n→π* peak for PNFs blue shifts (Fig. 5e). This result indicates electron transfer may occur from PNFs to the hydrolyzates of Fe3+.

As documented in literature [8,27], an electron transfer process is determined by whether the energy levels of the quencher match well with those of the host. Therefore, we measured the HOMO and LUMO levels of PNFs through the cyclic voltammetry (Fig. S2a). The results show the HOMO and LUMO levels of PNFs are located at ‒7.28 and ‒3.68 eV, respectively. Detailed results and discussion are provided in the Supplementary material.

The HOMO and LUMO levels of the ferric ion species were determined by theoretical computations, as depicted in Fig. 5f. The optimized configurations and HOMO‒LUMO diagrams are provided in Fig. S3. It is found that the LUMO level of Fe2(OH)2 4+ (‒5.84 eV) and two types of HOMO levels for Fe(OH)2 + (‒6.88 and ‒6.74 eV) are located between the HOMO and LUMO levels of PNFs. Because only single electron occupies the two types of HOMO level of Fe(OH)2 +, an excited electron on the LUMO level of PNFs can transfer to the LUMO level of Fe2(OH)2 4+and the HOMO levels of Fe(OH)2 + (Fig. 5f), resulting in fluorescence quenching of the host.

To confirm whether the electron transfer mechanism is suitable for interpreting the fluorescence quenching of CDs by ferric ions, N-containing CDs (NCDs) were prepared according to literature [27]. The HOMO‒LUMO levels of NCDs are estimated to be ca. ‒7.14 and ‒3.69 eV (Fig. S2b), close to those of PNFs. The absorption and PL emission spectra are provided in the Supplementary material (Fig. S4). Further, the PL response of NCDs towards metal ions, including Ag+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Pb2+, were measured as shown in Fig. S5. It is found that NCDs also exhibit high PL sensitivity to ferric ion. The good matching between the energy levels of NCDs and Fe3+ hydrolyzates (Fe2(OH)2 4+ and Fe(OH)2 +) indicates the electron transfer mechanism can be applied for interpreting the fluorescence quenching of CDs by ferric ions.

On the basis of the above analysis, the fluorescence quenching of PNFs by ferric ion in PNFs‒FN systems with FN concentration lower than 80 µM can be attributed to the electron transfer from PNFs to Fe2(OH)2 4+ and Fe(OH)2 + species, rather than the color reaction, electrostatic attraction, IFE, and FRET mechanisms. In PNFs‒FN systems with FN concentration higher than 80 µM, the IFE and FRET mechanisms cannot be excluded for the fluorescence quenching of PNFs by ferric ions.

In summary, a type of organic PNFs-based sensor is provided for fluorescent detection of ferric ion in mixed metal-ion solution and in real water. Five types of fluorescence quenching mechanisms concerning the luminophors with ferric ions have been explored that include color reaction, electrostatic interaction, IFE, FRET, and electron transfer. On the basis of instrumental analysis and theoretical computations, the fluorescence quenching of PNFs by ferric ion is attributed to the electron transfer mechanism for PNFs‒FN systems with FN concentration lower than 80 µM. This research provides deep understanding on the fluorescence quenching of carbon-based sensors by ferric ions.

There are considerable reports on fluorescent detection of ferric ion, which were universally simplified as Fe3+, by using carbon dots-based sensors. Although several mechanisms have been proposed for the fluorescence quenching of the host, it deserves deep understanding on this topic because the hydrolysis of Fe3+ has not been considered in previous reports. In this contribution, we focus on demonstration of the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of organic polymer nanofilms (PNFs) by ferric ions through instrumental analysis and theoretical calculations. The electron transfer from the LUMO level of PNFs to the LUMO level of Fe2(OH)2 4+and the HOMO levels of Fe(OH)2 + is suggested to be responsible for the impressive fluorescence quenching of PNFs by ferric ions with ferric ion concentration lower than 80 µM.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2019MEM015 and ZR2017QB003).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104639.
